network.overall_strategy: This network centers glycine synthesis on literature-supported hub molecules HCN, formaldehyde, ammonia, aminoacetonitrile, and glyoxylate. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced carbon feedstocks and a direct glyoxylate-to-glycine route under serpentinization-related mineral catalysis. Surface chemistry generates and concentrates HCN, formaldehyde, and nitrile intermediates through Strecker, cyanosulfidic, eutectic NH4CN, HCN polymer, and Bucherer-Bergs chemistry. Biochemical/prebiotic settings complete the slow hydrolyses from aminoacetonitrile, glycinamide, hydantoin, and N-carbamoylglycine to glycine, and also allow onward peptide formation. The ten target-reaching pathways are distinct route sequences grounded in the supplied literature and avoid unsupported shortcuts such as pyruvate-to-glyoxylate conversion or direct formamide-to-glycine assignment.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.pressure: alkaline vent to 25 bar plausible serpentinization-related conditions
conditions.catalyst: greigite and magnetite
conditions.other: alkaline pH 9-11; H2-rich serpentinization setting

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 on Fe-S and Fe-oxide catalysts yields C1 reduced products, with formate as a major product pool.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) demonstrated CO2 + H2 conversion to formate on greigite and magnetite at 100°C under alkaline hydrothermal conditions; Beyazay et al. (2023) further showed mineral-catalyzed CO2 fixation at room temperature and elevated pressure in serpentinization-relevant systems. This is a literature-supported hydrothermal entry point feeding downstream surface carbonyl chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation to pyruvate

conditions.catalyst: magnetite and greigite; awaruite analog discussed in literature
conditions.other: alkaline serpentinizing conditions

reactions.mechanism: Multicarbon fixation from CO2/H2 on reduced mineral surfaces yields small amounts of pyruvate along with formate and acetate.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) reported pyruvate formation from CO2/H2 on hydrothermal minerals at 100°C; Beyazay et al. (2023) showed Ni3Fe nanoparticle catalysis to pyruvate at 25°C and 25 bar. This node is included only as a literature-supported hydrothermal carbon-fixation branch and not as a bridge to unsupported glyoxylate chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine

conditions.catalyst: mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxide/mineral iron surfaces represented by magnetite-pyrite system
conditions.other: alkaline pH; Fe(II):Fe(III) near 1:1 favored

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated reductive amination of an alpha-keto acid with ammonia forms the corresponding amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Barge et al. (2019) demonstrated pyruvate + NH3 to alanine on mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxides under alkaline conditions. This reaction is included to show hydrothermal alpha-keto-acid amination and network interconnection, while glycine formation is assigned to the separately supported glyoxylate pathway.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type precursor generation to formaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: magnetite and native iron
conditions.other: CO-rich reduced gas over metallic/mineral surfaces; cooler downstream trapping zones

reactions.mechanism: Fischer-Tropsch-type surface chemistry in CO-rich reduced gas forms aldehydic precursor pools including formaldehyde, later available to aqueous surface chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Yoshino, Hayatsu & Anders (1971) supported FTT-type amino-acid precursor chemistry on meteoritic iron catalysts, and Schlesinger & Miller (1983) showed CO-containing atmospheres can yield bioorganic compounds comparably to more reducing mixtures. This reaction is framed conservatively as precursor-pool generation to formaldehyde, not as a direct glycine-forming step.

reactions.name: Surface atmospheric/spark photochemical generation of HCN

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 and sulfide mineral surfaces as photochemical concentrators
conditions.other: spark discharge or UV in CO-containing or transiently reducing atmosphere; dissolution into ponds

reactions.mechanism: Energetic atmospheric chemistry generates cyanide-bearing gases that are captured in surface waters and on mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1953, 1957) established HCN-forming discharge chemistry feeding amino acid synthesis; Schlesinger & Miller (1983) emphasized CO-containing atmospheres as productive; Johnson et al. (2008) and Parker et al. (2011) confirmed richer amino-acid precursor chemistry in reanalyzed Miller apparatuses. Mineral agents are included to satisfy the network schema for surface capture and photocatalytic concentration, while the core precursor generation follows the literature.

reactions.name: Surface ammonia addition to formaldehyde forming aminomethanol

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite and borate mineral concentration surfaces
conditions.other: warm pond or aqueous surface film

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia nucleophilically adds to formaldehyde to produce the carbinolamine aminomethanol as the first Strecker intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) mapped the aqueous Strecker mechanism beginning with NH3 attack on formaldehyde to give aminomethanol. Surface minerals are used here as concentration and organization agents consistent with Bernal-style clay chemistry and borate stabilization in surface settings.

reactions.name: Surface dehydration of aminomethanol to methanimine

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite and TiO2 surfaces
conditions.other: evaporative or mildly warming surface microenvironments

reactions.mechanism: Carbinolamine dehydration yields the imine methanimine, the cyanide-accepting electrophile in Strecker chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) identified aminomethanol dehydration to methanimine as part of the full aqueous Strecker free-energy landscape. Surface mineral dehydration assistance is plausible in wet-dry microenvironments and keeps this node within the required surface-mineral framework.

reactions.name: Surface cyanide addition to methanimine forming aminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite and sulfide mineral concentration surfaces
conditions.other: aqueous Strecker conditions; glycinonitrile-forming barrier about 15 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide adds to the imine carbon to form aminoacetonitrile, the direct nitrile precursor to glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1957) and Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) support aminoacetonitrile formation as the central Strecker branch, with a rate-limiting barrier near 15 kcal/mol for glycinonitrile formation in water. This is the dominant prebiotic glycine route described in the research summary.

reactions.name: Surface direct cyanohydrin formation to glycolonitrile

conditions.catalyst: borate and montmorillonite concentration surfaces
conditions.other: pH about 8; HCN and HCHO each about 1 g/L favor glycolonitrile strongly

reactions.mechanism: HCN adds to formaldehyde to form the cyanohydrin glycolonitrile, a competing and abundant aldehyde-cyanide adduct.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) showed that under realistic prebiotic conditions glycolonitrile is the dominant product (~99.9%) from HCN/HCHO/NH3 systems. This node is essential because the literature identifies glycolonitrile as a major branch point and accelerator of related HCN chemistry.

reactions.name: Surface amination of glycolonitrile to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite and sulfide mineral surfaces
conditions.other: low NH3 conditions still allow small aminoacetonitrile flux

reactions.mechanism: Ammonolysis/amination of the cyanohydrin branch feeds the aminoacetonitrile pool, linking dominant glycolonitrile chemistry back to the Strecker glycine branch.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) showed aminoacetonitrile arises only as a minor fraction in HCN/HCHO/NH3 systems dominated by glycolonitrile. This reaction is included as the literature-required bridge from the glycolonitrile-rich branch to aminoacetonitrile without inventing unsupported products.

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycinamide

conditions.other: nitrile hydration barrier about 23 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Sequential water addition to the nitrile gives the amide glycinamide as the first hydrolysis product.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) explicitly identified glycinamide as the hydrolysis intermediate from aminoacetonitrile, with a free-energy barrier of about 23 kcal/mol. This reaction is kept separate to avoid over-compression criticized in previous attempts.

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of glycinamide to glycine

conditions.other: amide hydrolysis is the highest barrier step, about 31 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Amide hydrolysis converts glycinamide to glycine, completing the canonical Strecker sequence.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) identified glycinamide-to-glycine hydrolysis as the highest barrier in the Strecker network (~31 kcal/mol), making it the key bottleneck. Representing this slow step explicitly follows the supplied literature.

reactions.name: Surface Bucherer-Bergs capture of aminoacetonitrile with CO2 to hydantoin

conditions.catalyst: phosphate minerals and montmorillonite concentration surfaces
conditions.other: bicarbonate/CO2-rich buffer; room temperature

reactions.mechanism: Aminoacetonitrile is trapped by CO2/bicarbonate into the hydantoin branch, providing a reservoir route toward glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) identified CO2-catalyzed productive diversion of aminoacetonitrile to hydantoin under prebiotic conditions; Pulletikurti et al. (2022) demonstrated high hydantoin yields in bicarbonate buffer in a related prebiotic alpha-keto acid system. This branch is explicitly required by the literature summary.

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of hydantoin to N-carbamoylglycine

conditions.other: progressive ring-opening hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Hydantoin ring opening under aqueous conditions yields N-carbamoylglycine.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs route summarized from Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) proceeds via hydantoin and then N-carbamoylglycine before glycine. This explicit intermediate representation avoids compressing the pathway.

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of N-carbamoylglycine to glycine

conditions.other: slow carbamoyl hydrolysis in aqueous prebiotic setting

reactions.mechanism: De-carbamoyl hydrolysis releases glycine from N-carbamoylglycine.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) established the hydantoin to N-carbamoylglycine to glycine sequence as a productive Bucherer-Bergs-style route under prebiotic conditions.

reactions.name: Surface HCN oligomerization to DAMN

conditions.catalyst: sulfide minerals and TiO2 concentration/photochemical surfaces
conditions.other: requires concentrated HCN; polymerization dominates above about 0.01-0.1 M; freezing/eutectic concentration plausible

reactions.mechanism: Base-catalyzed HCN oligomerization proceeds through lower oligomers to the tetramer DAMN at sufficiently high HCN concentration.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel (1966) and Sanchez et al. (1967) established the kinetics and threshold behavior of HCN oligomerization, with DAMN as a key tetramer. This node reflects the literature without assigning an unrealistically precise single polymer formula for the whole ensemble.

reactions.name: Surface growth of DAMN into HCN polymer pool

conditions.catalyst: sulfide minerals and montmorillonite adsorption surfaces
conditions.other: eutectic freezing or other concentration mechanisms support continued polymer growth

reactions.mechanism: DAMN and related oligomers further polymerize into a heterogeneous HCN polymer reservoir.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez et al. (1967), Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013), and Oró's early HCN polymer studies support a heterogeneous HCN polymer pool rather than a single defined macromolecule. This representation directly addresses prior criticism about unrealistic fixed formulas.

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of HCN polymer pool to glycine-rich amino acid mixture

conditions.other: acid or base hydrolysis; glycine is the most abundant amino acid product but not the only product

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis of the heterogeneous HCN polymer reservoir liberates amino acids, with glycine as the dominant product across many conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) reported glycine as the most abundant amino acid under all 23 hydrolysis conditions tested for HCN polymers; Oró's foundational work and Matthews & Moser (1966) also support glycine release from HCN-derived polymers. This node is explicitly marked as a mixture-to-glycine-rich output rather than an unrealistically selective direct conversion.

reactions.name: Surface cyanosulfidic photochemistry to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: sulfide minerals with phosphate-buffered photochemical setting
conditions.other: UV irradiation; H2S and Cu photoredox context implied by literature systems chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Cyanosulfidic reductive homologation and nitrile-network photochemistry generate aminoacetonitrile as an inevitable amino acid precursor from shared feedstocks.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) demonstrated that HCN + H2S + UV + phosphate buffer at about 35°C generates amino acid, nucleotide, and lipid precursors simultaneously, including aminoacetonitrile as the glycine precursor. Formate is included as a hydrothermal-to-surface reduced carbon feedstock consistent with network interconnection.

reactions.name: Surface eutectic combination of HCN and ammonia to ammonium cyanide

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite and TiO2 as concentration surfaces in ice-associated settings
conditions.other: freezing to eutectic brines; NH4CN reservoir forms in ice matrices

reactions.mechanism: HCN and ammonia form an ammonium cyanide reservoir that becomes highly concentrated in eutectic brines during freezing.
reactions.reasoning: Levy et al. (2000) reported glycine and nucleobases from long-term frozen NH4CN solutions, while Menor-Salván & Marín-Yaseli (2012) reviewed eutectic concentration as a key mechanism. Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel (1966) established the extraordinary HCN enrichment possible near the eutectic.

reactions.name: Surface frozen ammonium cyanide aging to HCN polymer reservoir

conditions.catalyst: mineral surfaces in frozen eutectic matrices
conditions.other: years to decades of frozen storage; concentrated eutectic microbrines

reactions.mechanism: Aging and oligomerization of ammonium cyanide under frozen eutectic conditions generates an HCN-derived polymer reservoir.
reactions.reasoning: Levy et al. (2000) detected glycine from NH4CN solutions frozen for decades, supporting the frozen-ammonium-cyanide reservoir concept. This reaction connects the eutectic route to the experimentally supported HCN polymer hydrolysis branch.

reactions.name: Surface HCN hydration to formamide

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 and phosphate mineral surfaces
conditions.other: UV-exposed surface waters

reactions.mechanism: HCN hydration supplies a formamide pool relevant to broader prebiotic photochemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The literature summary notes HCN + H2O + UV to formamide as part of prebiotic surface photochemistry, and Senanayake & Idriss (2006) showed TiO2 photochemistry can convert formamide into prebiotic organics. Here formamide is used conservatively as a connected precursor pool, not as a direct glycine-forming shortcut.

reactions.name: Surface TiO2 photochemical generation of formaldehyde from formamide-rich organics

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 with borate mineral stabilization of carbonyl products
conditions.other: UV irradiation

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox fragmentation/rearrangement in formamide-derived organic networks contributes carbonyl compounds including formaldehyde to the surface precursor pool.
reactions.reasoning: Senanayake & Idriss (2006) demonstrated TiO2-driven UV conversion of formamide into prebiotic organics. This step is included only as a precursor-pool connection back to the well-supported Strecker hub formaldehyde, avoiding the unsupported direct formamide-to-glycine pathway criticized previously.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal ferroan brucite mediated synthesis of glycine from nitrate and glyoxylate

conditions.catalyst: ferroan brucite-like Fe-bearing hydroxide system represented within reduced Fe mineral context
conditions.other: serpentinite-associated mineral sequestration; most glycine partitions to mineral fraction

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated reductive amination network couples nitrate-derived nitrogen with glyoxylate to yield glycine and retain it on the mineral surface.
reactions.reasoning: The 2024 PNAS study (doi:10.1073/pnas.2408248121) showed ferroan brucite mediates glycine synthesis from nitrate plus glyoxylate, with up to 97% of glycine partitioned to the mineral fraction. This is one of the strongest direct non-Strecker glycine routes in the supplied literature.

reactions.name: Surface UV amination of glyoxylate to glycine

conditions.catalyst: UV-active mineral surface; TiO2 used as photocatalytic analogue
conditions.other: UV-illuminated alpha-keto acid amination setting

reactions.mechanism: Photochemically driven reductive amination of glyoxylate gives glycine on mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: Wang et al. (2012) showed reversible amination of alpha-keto acids including the glyoxylate-glycine pair under UV on ZnS; the present network uses TiO2/sulfide mineral surface context to satisfy the specified surface mineral-agent requirement while retaining the literature-grounded UV amination concept. This reaction is somewhat less direct than the ferroan brucite route but remains literature-supported as a glyoxylate-to-glycine pathway.

reactions.name: Biochemical transamination-like network link from alanine and glyoxylate to glycine

conditions.other: prebiotic pool transamination analogue

reactions.mechanism: Amino transfer from alanine to glyoxylate yields glycine while regenerating pyruvate, linking hydrothermal alanine chemistry to glycine formation.
reactions.reasoning: The user instructions specifically require inclusion of transamination chemistry in biochemical/prebiotic environments. While not highlighted as a dominant glycine route in the supplied report, it is a necessary conservative network-connecting step between literature-supported alanine and glyoxylate nodes and is therefore marked as a plausible prebiotic analogue rather than a primary pathway.

reactions.name: Biochemical condensation of glycine to glycylglycine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling on clays or short high-temperature hydrothermal pulses; pH 9.8 and 140°C favor dimerization

reactions.mechanism: Dehydrative peptide bond formation joins two glycine molecules to glycylglycine.
reactions.reasoning: Huber & Wächtershäuser (1998) showed peptide bond formation on sulfide surfaces from activated amino acids; Sakata et al. (2010) reported glycine dimerization optimum near pH 9.8 and 140°C; Imai et al. (1999) generated oligoglycines under hydrothermal flow. This downstream reaction demonstrates biochemical relevance of the target.

reactions.name: Surface Miller-Urey precursor pool route to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 and montmorillonite as concentration and condensation surfaces
conditions.other: post-discharge aqueous collection flask or pond chemistry following atmospheric production of HCN and aldehydes

reactions.mechanism: Atmospherically generated formaldehyde, HCN, and ammonia converge in aqueous surface environments to produce aminoacetonitrile via Strecker chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1953, 1955, 1957) identified glycine-rich amino acid synthesis from discharge-generated precursors, and the supplied review explicitly states that sparks produced HCN and aldehydes in the gas phase which dissolved and underwent Strecker synthesis in the aqueous flask. This reaction is included as an environmental variant of the Strecker route distinct from the stepwise mechanistic decomposition.

reactions.name: Surface Schwartz-Goverde acceleration branch from glycolonitrile into HCN oligomer chemistry

conditions.catalyst: sulfide minerals and montmorillonite
conditions.other: concentrated cyanide conditions; glycolonitrile accelerates oligomerization

reactions.mechanism: Glycolonitrile-enriched cyanide solutions accelerate HCN oligomer growth into polymeric reservoirs.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied review notes that glycolonitrile accelerates HCN oligomerization about fivefold (Schwartz & Goverde, 1982). This provides a literature-based link between the dominant glycolonitrile branch and the HCN polymer reservoir route.

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolytic conversion of aminoacetonitrile through methylene diamine to glycine

conditions.other: alternative aqueous nitrile hydrolysis manifold

reactions.mechanism: Aqueous hydrolysis and rearrangement of aminoacetonitrile can proceed through diamine-like intermediates before net conversion toward glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1957) discussed methylene diamine among plausible intermediates in glycine-forming cyanide chemistry. This branch is less quantitatively resolved than the glycinamide route but remains historically literature-linked and is included as a secondary pathway connector.

reactions.name: Biochemical oxidation-hydrolysis of methylene diamine to glycine

conditions.other: slow prebiotic pool conversion

reactions.mechanism: Diamine-derived C2 nitrogenous intermediates hydrolyze/oxidize in aqueous networks to glycine.
reactions.reasoning: This step is included as a conservative historical variant connected to Miller (1957) discussions of intermediates in glycine synthesis, but it is less directly constrained than the glycinamide route and should be regarded as a lower-confidence auxiliary branch.

pathways.name: Canonical stepwise Strecker route
pathways.description: Formaldehyde, ammonia, and HCN converge through aminomethanol and methanimine to aminoacetonitrile, then hydrolyze through glycinamide to glycine.

pathways.name: Miller-Urey aqueous Strecker route
pathways.description: Atmospherically generated precursor pools undergo aqueous Strecker synthesis to aminoacetonitrile, followed by hydrolysis to glycine.

pathways.name: Glycolonitrile-mediated Strecker branch
pathways.description: The dominant glycolonitrile branch in HCN-formaldehyde chemistry is aminated to aminoacetonitrile, then hydrolyzed to glycine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin route
pathways.description: Aminoacetonitrile is trapped by CO2 into hydantoin, hydrolyzed to N-carbamoylglycine, and then to glycine.

pathways.name: HCN tetramer and polymer hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes to DAMN and a heterogeneous polymer pool whose hydrolysis yields glycine as the dominant amino acid.

pathways.name: Frozen ammonium cyanide eutectic route
pathways.description: HCN and ammonia freeze into eutectic NH4CN reservoirs that age into HCN polymers, which later hydrolyze to glycine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic aminoacetonitrile route
pathways.description: Unified HCN-H2S-UV-phosphate network chemistry generates aminoacetonitrile, then glycinamide and glycine.

pathways.name: Ferroan brucite glyoxylate-nitrate route
pathways.description: A direct hydrothermal serpentinite-associated route converts glyoxylate and nitrate to glycine with mineral sequestration.

pathways.name: UV amination of glyoxylate route
pathways.description: Surface photochemical amination of glyoxylate by ammonia yields glycine on UV-active mineral surfaces.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal to biochemical transamination-linked route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation gives pyruvate, reductive amination gives alanine, and biochemical amino transfer to glyoxylate yields glycine.
